Mission Control: Tracking Aurora-3

This summer, you were selected to join the Mission Control Internship Program studying how satellites move around Earth at a local observatory.

As a Junior Orbital Response Specialist, your mission is to investigate satellite observations, analyze movement patterns, and help scientists better understand orbital motion.

Mission Control: Tracking Aurora-3

This summer, you were selected to join the Mission Control Internship Program, working alongside scientists and satellite tracking specialists connected to your town's local observatory

Your internship mentor, Maya Chen, is part of the orbital tracking team that studies how satellites move around Earth.

As one of the Junior Orbital Response Specialist interns, you will help analyze satellite observations, investigate movement patterns, and support ongoing orbital research.

Maya welcomes you and the other interns into Mission Control.

"Our teams study how satellites move through space and how they stay in orbit around Earth" Maya explains.
"Today, you and your fellow interns will investigate Aurora-3 and study the patterns scientists use to track orbital movement"
Section 1 of 3
Mission Control Alert

Maya pulls the newest orbital tracking report onto the main screen.

"This is Aurora-3," Maya says. "It's a weather research satellite that travels around Earth collecting information about clouds, storms, and the atmosphere."
"Our Mission Control teams use tracking data like this to study how satellites move around Earth and remain in orbit over time."

Our Mission Control systems track:

  • Aurora-3’s orbital path around Earth
  • changes in position over time
  • movement and direction during orbit

Our scientists study these observations to better understand how satellites travel around Earth and stay in orbit over time.

Maya zooms in on the satellite’s orbital path.

"The Junior Orbital Response Specialist interns are going to help analyze the observation data, look for patterns, and investigate how Aurora-3 moves around Earth."
Section 2 of 3

Different Mission Control team members are beginning their investigations from different starting points. As a Junior Orbital Response Specialist intern, you will join and assist a team.

First, you must decide which team to join and where to begin analyzing the observation data.

Maya organizes the interns around the Mission Control observation screens.

“Each team is studying a different part of Aurora-3’s orbital data,” Maya explains.
"Choose where you would like to begin investigating orbital movement."
Decide where you will begin.
Mission Control collected orbital tracking observations across multiple observation cycles for your team to analyze.
Each path helps your team investigate how Aurora-3 moves around Earth.
Section 3 of 3
Orbit Tracking Team

Maya leads you to the orbital tracking station.

Large observation screens display archived tracking cycles showing Aurora-3 moving around Earth over time.

In this system, each cycle shows one full orbit around Earth.

Mission Control teams study these observation cycles to better understand how satellites travel around Earth and remain in orbit.

Maya points to the tracking display.

"You will study the shape and pattern of Aurora-3’s orbit," Maya explains.
"As you review each observation cycle, look carefully for patterns that repeat over time."

You open the orbital tracking system and begin reviewing Aurora-3’s observation data.

◈ Mission Control · Orbit Observation Terminal · Aurora-3
ARCHIVED
📓 Journal Prompt — Orbit Tracking Team

Journal Reflection:

What patterns do you notice about how Aurora-3 moves around Earth?

Use evidence from the observation cycles to describe:

  • how the satellite’s path curves around Earth
  • what patterns repeat over time
  • what you noticed about the orbit’s shape
Responses are saved automatically as you type.
Section 4a of 7
Motion Analysis Team

Maya guides you to a Mission Control station filled with movement diagrams and orbital tracking displays.

Lines and arrows move across the screens as Aurora-3 travels around Earth.

In this system, each cycle shows one full orbit around Earth.

Mission Control teams study movement data like this to understand how satellites move during orbit and how their direction changes over time.

Maya studies the tracking display carefully.

"You will focus on Aurora-3’s movement during orbit," Maya explains.
"As you review the observation cycles, pay close attention to how the satellite moves and changes direction around Earth."

You begin reviewing Aurora-3’s archived movement observations.

◈ Mission Control · Motion Analysis Terminal · Aurora-3
ARCHIVED
📓 Journal Prompt — Motion Analysis Team

Journal Reflection:

What patterns do you notice about Aurora-3’s movement during orbit?

Use evidence from the observation cycles to describe:

  • how the satellite moves around Earth
  • how its direction changes during orbit
  • what movement patterns repeat over time
Responses are saved automatically as you type.
Section 4b of 7
Mission Control Team Briefing

The Junior Orbital Response Specialist interns regroup around the main observation display.

Maya combines the orbital tracking and movement observations onto the central screen.

Different investigation teams noticed different patterns in Aurora-3’s movement around Earth.

Some teams focused on the shape and repetition of the orbit.

Other teams focused on how Aurora-3 moved and changed direction during orbit.

Maya studies the combined observations carefully.

"Even though your teams studied different parts of the tracking data, many of your observations connect," Maya explains.
"Mission Control scientists often combine evidence from multiple investigations to better understand what they are observing."

You begin reviewing the combined observation findings from both investigation groups.

Section 5 of 7
Why Does Aurora-3 Stay in Orbit?

The Mission Control teams continue studying the combined observation data.

Maya stands quietly near the main tracking display as the repeated orbit cycles continue across the screen.

Aurora-3 keeps moving forward around Earth, yet its path continues curving instead of traveling away into space.

The repeated movement patterns raise new questions for the investigation teams.

"If Aurora-3 keeps moving forward," Maya says, "something must be affecting its motion."
"But what could cause a satellite to keep curving around Earth instead of flying away into space?"
"Mission Control scientists investigate questions like these to better understand how satellites remain in orbit."

Your team prepares to investigate possible explanations for Aurora-3’s movement.

Choose Your Next Investigation
Mission Control scientists now need to investigate what may be affecting Aurora-3’s movement around Earth.
Different investigation teams will explore possible explanations for the satellite’s repeated orbit patterns.

📖 Mission Glossary
Orbit
Placeholder. The curved path an object takes around another object in space due to gravity. Replace with your lesson-specific definition.
Gravity
Placeholder. The force that pulls objects toward one another. Replace with your lesson-specific definition.
Inertia
Placeholder. The tendency of an object to keep moving unless a force acts on it. Replace with your lesson-specific definition.
Aurora-3
Placeholder. The research satellite your team is investigating. It was designed to study weather patterns and atmospheric changes around Earth.
Mission Control
Placeholder. The team on the ground that monitors and guides spacecraft and satellites. Replace with your lesson-specific definition.
